title: Foodie Play Kitchen Stove Wood Toy
summary: A toy stove with a plexiglass oven window and wood-round burner knobs — every joint gets reinforced, since this is a cabinet built to survive a toddler's daily use.
description:
The source is explicit that every joint on this build gets reinforced, and that's worth taking seriously — a toy stove gets more abuse per square inch than almost any piece of furniture in the house, between a kid climbing on it, slamming the door, and spinning the knobs. Building it square from the very first step and rechecking square at each stage isn't a nice-to-have here; a rack in the box shows up immediately as a door that won't close evenly.

The plexiglass oven window is what makes this read as a real appliance instead of a plain box with a door — screwed in with just one screw per corner, it's simple to install but adds real visual payoff. Wood rounds for burner knobs and scrap-wood "grates" nailed onto the top are both details that take minutes but do most of the work of making the stovetop legible as a stovetop.

materials.name: Wood dowel pieces, 1" long
materials.note: Leftover closet rod pieces work well.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the 8 ft 1x12 (actual 3/4" x 11 1/4") cut the top at 22", two sides at 20" and the bottom at 20 1/2" — 82 1/2" of parts, so one board covers the carcass. From the 8 ft 1x4 cut the front top rail at 22", two door frame sides at 14 1/2" and two door frame top/bottom pieces at 15"; that is 81" and again fits the one board. Cut the front bottom rail 22" long from the 2 ft 1x3, and the stovetop grate strip 18 1/2" long from the 1x2. Cut the 1/4" plywood back 22" x 20 3/4", which is the exact outside face of the finished box and will be your squaring check later. Square every cut and label the pieces; the 20" sides and the 20 1/2" bottom are close enough in length to mix up on a bench.

steps.title: Build the basic box
steps.body: The two 20" sides stand vertically and the 20 1/2" bottom fits between them, which makes the carcass 22" wide outside — exactly the length of the top, which then lands across both sides. Assemble with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" brad nails, or 1 1/4" brads and glue, and put glue in every joint: the source is explicit that every joint on this build gets reinforced, and a toy stove takes more abuse per square inch than anything else in the house. Check both diagonals across the box before the glue sets, and recheck after each of the next two steps — a rack in the carcass shows up immediately as an oven door that will not close evenly, and it is unfixable once the back is on.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: The 22" x 20 3/4" plywood back matches the box's outside face exactly, so line one corner up, nail that edge, then pull the opposite corner into line with the panel's edge before nailing the rest — the panel squares the carcass permanently. Use the shorter 3/4" screws or brads here; a 1 1/4" fastener driven into the 3/4" edge of a side tends to split out and buys you nothing on a 1/4" back. Nail into the sides, the top and the bottom every few inches, with glue behind it. Check the diagonals one last time before the last edge goes down.

steps.title: Attach the front pieces
steps.body: The two front rails are both 22", the full outside width of the box. The 3 1/2"-wide 1x4 goes on at the top of the front opening and the 2 1/2"-wide 1x3 at the bottom, and between them they leave about 14 3/4" of clear front opening for the oven door. Set both with their ends flush to the outside faces of the sides, and fasten with 1 1/4" brads and glue into the side edges and into the top and bottom boards. Check for square again before the glue grabs, and measure the actual opening you have ended up with — the door is built to that number, not to the list.

steps.title: Build and hang the oven door
steps.body: The door is a frame of 3 1/2"-wide 1x4: two 14 1/2" sides with the two 15" top and bottom pieces between them, which makes a full-overlay door 22" wide by 14 1/2" tall — the same 22" as the box — with a 15" x 7 1/2" window opening in the middle. Build it flat with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" brad nails plus glue, checking the diagonals, since a racked door will never sit right against a square box. Lay the 8" x 16" plexiglass over the opening from behind; it overlaps about 1/2" all around, so drill a clearance hole (slightly oversize, so the plastic can move without cracking) in each corner and drive one 3/4" wood screw per corner — four screws is all it needs. Hang the door on the surface-mount self-closing hinges, and expect roughly 1/4" of total clearance between the two front rails, so trim the door's top or bottom edge slightly if the hinges need more.

steps.title: Add feet (optional)
steps.body: Feet lift the stove off the floor and give it an appliance-like stance, and they also keep the bottom board off a damp floor. Cut four short blocks from the 1x2 and fasten them up into the bottom board near each corner with glue and screws, setting them in far enough from the edges that a kid pushing on the top corner cannot tip the stove over its own foot. Make all four the same length — cut them with a stop block — and check the finished stove for rock on a flat floor before anything else goes on.

steps.title: Build the stovetop
steps.body: Cut the 18 1/2" grate strip into short pieces and lay them out on the 22" x 11 1/4" top in two burner-sized groups, spacing them so each group reads as a grate; move them around dry until the layout looks right before any glue goes down. Nail them with 1 1/4" brads and glue, driving into the top board and keeping the nails away from the ends of the short pieces so they do not split. Then set the four burner knobs: glue a 1" dowel piece to the back of each 2" wood round, mark the four knob positions across the front top rail evenly and on one level line, and fasten each dowel into the rail. Sand every edge and corner of the finished stove well — this is a toy, so nothing should be sharp anywhere a child can reach.

images.alt: Wood toy play kitchen stove with plexiglass oven window
